remarks:
TEOSS1 recording. Aberrant TRISYLLABIC echemes, observed in a proportion of 30% aberrantly singing individuals to 70% individuals delivering quadrisyllabic echemes. Subjectively, the trisyllabic echems feel muffled and dull. High Pass Filtered at 1 kHz to cutoff wind and sound of steps of passers by.
Considering the season and the elevation, Filippo Buzzetti hypotesized that youngest G. campestris of this population emit trisyllabic echemes, fully grown specimens a regular quadrisyllabic echemes.

remarks:

Band pass filtered above 2800 Hz and below 45000 Hz to suppress wind and to include only the band where the song is present.

location: Ricovero pecore presso bosco torbiera Viote Bondone
remarks:
According to the authoritative opinion of Baudewijn Odé (who listened to the song played in low quality on my tablet) this resembles too closely the song of G. campestris to be anything else.
I observe that the syllables are shorter, less clear and with a more metallic timbre than usual for G. campestris, but most of all that the echemes include five syllables instead of four.
A song of a nearby G. campestris taken in the same hours ( https://xeno-canto.org/918970 ) is available for comparison.
Band-passed above 2500 Hz and below 90000 Hz to suppress the wind noise without affecting the band in which the song is apparent.
